item.title: ‘Widow’s Bay’ became a hit the old-fashioned way
item.description: Word of mouth, not the algorithm, turned Apple TV's horror-comedy into appointment viewing.

item.title: How young MAGA voters view America’s role in the world
item.description: From Ukraine to Iran to Taiwan, Trump supporters under 30 favor engagement over isolation.

item.title: School choice for me, but not for thee
item.description: These senators picked private schools for their kids. Why stop others from choosing?

item.title: Don’t let Trump’s golf course plan wreck D.C.’s perfect running route
item.description: Golf isn’t the only form of recreation that takes place in East Potomac Park.

item.title: Who vetted Graham Platner? Anyone?
item.description: Democrats wanted a fighter badly enough to sacrifice their standards.

item.title: The Supreme Court just handed Republicans a midterm lifeline
item.description: Unlimited coordinated spending means cheap ad rates — and a chance to erase Democrats’ cash edge.

item.title: One state epitomizes the reason America lacks starter homes
item.description: Young people are fleeing. Seniors are stuck. Massachusetts needs more starter homes.

item.title: China’s ‘hustlers’ are sloppy. That doesn’t make them ineffective.
item.description: Eileen Wang’s arrest highlights the contradictory nature of Chinese influence campaigns.

item.title: I rejected the religion of sports. Here’s what converted me.
item.description: A lifelong sports skeptic learns to love the power of the game.

item.title: What a viral photo of white supremacists on the Metro reveals
item.description: An extremist group exploits the very democracy it opposes.

item.title: How populists turn distrust into power
item.description:
Populist candidates have a way of making politics feel simple. They tell voters that the problem is obvious, the villains are known and the solution is to break through corrupt institutions standing in the way. That message can be emotionally powerful, but what happens after populists win? Why do they so often win the crowd but lose the plot?
Host Megan McArdle is joined by Richard Hanania, a political scientist and writer whose ideological journey has taken many turns. His new book, "Kakistocracy," argues that populism tends to produce worse leaders, weaker institutions, more corruption and a politics driven by resentment and conspiratorial thinking. They discuss why populism is so appealing and whether liberal democracy can rebuild trust.

item.title: Trump’s FIFA call was unfair to U.S. soccer fans
item.description: Folarin Balogun's suspension reversal, America 250 celebrations and more, in readers' eyes.
